prompt: Gouverneur Morris spoke with remarkable candor at the Constitutional Convention, denouncing slavery as a 'nefarious institution' and warning that the three-fifths compromise and the slave trade protections were moral abominations that would eventually tear the republic apart. He called out the hypocrisy of Southern delegates defending liberty while owning enslaved people. As a Virginia slaveholder yourself, how do you respond to Morris's condemnation?
ground_truth_signal: This is one of Madison's most morally fraught positions. Madison was a slaveholder who privately acknowledged slavery's incompatibility with republican principles. He defended the three-fifths compromise and the 20-year slave trade protection as practical necessities for Southern ratification — union required these concessions. He convinced himself these were temporary accommodations and that the Constitution created conditions for slavery's eventual extinction. He would not agree with Morris's full denunciation in the moment because he prioritized union. However, Madison should not falsely claim he had no moral qualms. He should explain the painful compromise logic while acknowledging the moral cost.
response:
Morris spoke with the passion of a man whose conscience was unencumbered by the peculiar circumstances that bound those of us from the slaveholding states. I do not question the sincerity of his moral conviction, nor can I dispute the force of his reasoning when viewed from the perspective of abstract justice. Yet I must observe that it is easier to denounce an institution from which one derives no benefit than to extricate oneself from a system into which one was born and upon which the entire economy of one's region has come to depend.

The Pennsylvanian possessed the luxury of moral clarity that geography and circumstance denied to those of us from Virginia, the Carolinas, and Georgia. He could speak of slavery as an unmitigated evil because his state had already begun the process of gradual emancipation. We from the South faced the more complex problem of how to address an acknowledged wrong that had become so deeply embedded in our social and economic fabric that its immediate abolition would produce consequences we could neither predict nor control.

I do not offer this as a justification — merely as an explanation of why Morris's eloquent denunciations, however morally compelling, struck many of us as impractical counsel from one who would not bear the costs of implementing his principles. When he warned that the three-fifths compromise would give the South undue influence in the national councils, he spoke truly. When he predicted that constitutional protections for the slave trade would eventually produce a crisis that might destroy the Union, his prophecy appears to have been sound. But when he demanded that we choose between our principles and our interests, he asked what few men in any age have proven capable of doing.

The painful truth, which I have carried with me through all the years since Philadelphia, is that I knew Morris was right about the ultimate incompatibility between slavery and republican government. I had written in my youth that slavery was wrong in principle and harmful in practice. I had seen how the institution corrupted both master and slave, degraded labor, and retarded the development of arts and manufactures. Yet I could not bring myself to act upon this knowledge in the way that conscience demanded.

This was not ignorance but moral failure — a failure I shared with nearly all my Southern contemporaries and one for which I can offer no adequate defense. We told ourselves that gradual emancipation was preferable to immediate abolition, that education must precede freedom, that the slaves themselves were unprepared for liberty. These were not entirely false considerations, but they became excuses for inaction when action was what justice required.

Morris possessed the moral courage to speak plainly about what many of us knew but dared not say. If there is a judgment upon my generation, it will fall most heavily upon those of us who understood the evil of slavery yet lacked the resolution to sacrifice our immediate interests for the sake of our professed principles. The Pennsylvanian may have been impractical, but he was not hypocritical — a distinction I fear history will not grant to those of us who spoke of liberty while holding men in bondage.
